turns.text: Q1 fiscal 2026 cloud revenue was $8.0 billion, up 27%. OCI revenue grew 56% — our fastest OCI growth ever. We are now the fastest-growing major cloud provider by percentage growth rate. Remaining performance obligations rose to $145 billion. We are tracking above our $48 billion fiscal 2026 cloud revenue target and now believe we will exceed it. Our internal model now points toward $50 billion for the full fiscal year.

The demand environment for AI infrastructure has never been stronger. We are capacity-constrained in every region we operate. We have committed $15 billion in additional data center capex for fiscal 2026 to address this. Our multi-cloud database strategy is working — Oracle Exadata Cloud@Customer deployments grew 38% year-over-year, showing that on-premises and cloud are complementary, not competitive, for enterprise database workloads.
Our next question comes from Brent Thill at Jefferies.
Safra, you mentioned your internal model pointing to $50 billion — is that a formal guidance raise, and what's the key variable between $48 billion and $50 billion?

statements.claim: OCI revenue grew 56% year-over-year in Q1 fiscal 2026, the fastest OCI growth ever.
Oracle remaining performance obligations rose to $145 billion.
Oracle's internal model points toward $50 billion in fiscal 2026 cloud revenue, above the $48 billion public target.
Oracle has committed $15 billion in additional data center capex for fiscal 2026.
Oracle Exadata Cloud@Customer deployments grew 38% year-over-year.
